errors: The implementation does not specify any clinically relevant parameters. There are no age thresholds, no look‑back time intervals, and no value sets defined for conditions or medications. Without these essential criteria, the decision support logic cannot be evaluated for clinical reasonableness.

; Age upper limit is set to **< 24 years**, which excludes 24‑year‑old patients. Most clinical practice guidelines (e.g., CDC) recommend screening for individuals **≤ 24 years** (i.e., up to and including age 24). The ELM should use “Age ≤ 24” or “Age < 25” to align with the guideline.; The value set for **“Reason for not performing Chlamydia Screening”** is listed as **TBD** (to be determined). The lookback period for a prior colonoscopy is set to **1 month**, which is clinically inappropriate. Standard colon cancer screening guidelines consider a colonoscopy valid for **10 years** (or 10 years from the date of the procedure) when determining eligibility for repeat screening. The age threshold (≥ 50 years) and the value set identifier are appropriate, but the time interval does not align with accepted clinical practice.

; Age threshold is set to ≥ 50 years, but most current prostate‑cancer screening guidelines (e.g., USPSTF) recommend shared decision‑making for men aged 55‑69 and do not endorse routine screening starting at 50 years for average‑risk patients.; The look‑back interval for the assessment is only 1 month; clinical practice typically uses a 12‑month (or longer) look‑back period to determine whether a PSA test, DRE, or biopsy has been performed recently. A 1‑month window is too short to capture standard screening intervals.

; Age threshold is set to ≥ 25 years, but most hypertension screening guidelines recommend initiating screening at age 18 (or ≥ 20) for the general adult population. The higher threshold would miss younger adults who should be screened.; The look‑back period of 6 months is shorter than the typical annual (12‑month) screening interval recommended for average‑risk adults; a 6‑month interval may be overly frequent unless the patient has elevated risk factors.; The value set OID (2.16.840.1.113883.3.600.1920) correctly identifies the “Encounter to Screen for Blood Pressure” set.

The implementation lists a “Time Interval (Lookback Period)” value of **190 mg/dL**, which is a laboratory measurement (LDL‑cholesterol level) rather than a temporal interval (e.g., “1 year” or “12 months”). Lookback periods should be expressed in units of time, not concentration. This mismatch indicates an error in how the value was categorized in the ELM.

The “Time Intervals (Lookback Periods)” entry lists **“9 g%”**, which is a laboratory value (HbA1c percentage) rather than a temporal duration. Look‑back periods should be expressed in units of time (e.g., days, months, years). This mismatch indicates that the ELM incorrectly uses a clinical measurement as a time interval. All other entries (Age ≥ 18 years and the HbA1c value set identifier) are appropriate.

; **Age Threshold** – The CDS uses a minimum age of ≥ 60 years, but current colorectal cancer screening guidelines (e.g., USPSTF 2021) recommend initiating average‑risk screening at age 45 years (previously 50 years). The lower bound is therefore higher than recommended.; **Lookback Period** – A 2‑year lookback for “Has Recent Colorectal Screening” does not align with the evidence‑based intervals for the primary screening modalities: colonoscopy (10 years), FIT/FOBT (1 year), and flexible sigmoidoscopy (5 years). A uniform 2‑year interval may miss patients who are up‑to‑date with colonoscopy or incorrectly flag those who are only 2 years out from a FIT.

; **Age Threshold:** The implementation uses an age threshold of ≥ 30 years, whereas current CDC and USPSTF guidelines recommend HIV screening for all individuals aged 13–64 (or 15–65) years. The threshold is therefore too restrictive.; **Lookback Period:** A 1‑year lookback for a recent HIV test is appropriate only for high‑risk populations. General population screening guidelines advise at least one test in a lifetime (or repeat testing no more frequently than every 3–5 years for low‑risk individuals). The 1‑year interval is therefore not aligned with standard recommendations.; **Value Set:** The HIV test value set OID (2.16.840.1.113762.1.4.1056.50) appears appropriate.

; The “Time Intervals (Lookback Periods)” section incorrectly includes a BMI threshold (35 kg/m²) as a time interval; BMI is a clinical measurement threshold, not a look‑back period.; The look‑back period for a recent glucose test is listed as “1 years”; the correct phrasing should be “1 year”.; All other values (age thresholds and value set OIDs) are appropriate.

Age threshold set to ≥ 25 years is higher than standard recommendations (e.g., USPSTF advises alcohol misuse screening for all adults ≥ 18 years). This would miss younger adults who should be screened. The look‑back period of 1 year and the value set OID are appropriate.

The lookback period for a recent DEXA scan is set to 6 months, which is clinically too short. Current osteoporosis screening guidelines typically recommend repeating a DEXA scan no more frequently than every 2 years (24 months) unless there is a specific clinical indication. All other values (age threshold of ≥65 years for women, the listed value set OIDs for female sex, osteoporosis, osteopenia, osteoporotic fractures, and DEXA) are appropriate.

The lower age threshold is set to ≥ 55 years, but guideline recommendations for abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) screening specify a starting age of 65 years (typically 65–75 years). The upper age limit of ≤ 75 years is appropriate. Additionally, the guideline includes a gender criterion (screen men, not women), which is not reflected in the current ELM implementation.
